coredata.dc:title: Amplified electrochemiluminescence of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ via coreactant active sites on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Lingyue
Li, Libo
Luo, Lijun
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Searching for new alternative to tripropylamine (TPrA) with low toxicity and high chemical stability for the tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+) based coreactant electrochemiluminescence (ECL) system is essential for widespread analytical applications. Here, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been discovered to significantly amplify the ECL emission and increase the ECL efficiency of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ for the first time. However, the mechanism by which NGQDs act as coreactants is not well comprehended. Therefore, various optical and electrochemical technologies were employed to investigate the ECL mechanism. It is proposed that the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of NGQDs play crucial roles as the coreactant active sites, catalyzing the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Based on this foundation, an “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor for the quantification of acetamiprid was developed, exhibiting a broad linear range and a detection limit of 0.056 pM. Satisfactory recoveries, ranging from 98.0 % to 101.6 %, were achieved in pakchoi samples. Consequently, NGQDs could serve as coreactants for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, offering new opportunities for constructing a variety of sensors with extensive analytical applications in the ECL field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ru(dcbpy)3 2+
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Amplified electrochemiluminescence
Coreactant active sites
Acetamiprid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL properties of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system and the possible mechanism
Fabrication and characteristic of the “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor
Analytical performance of the constructed aptasensor
Determination of acetamiprid in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)XPS spectrum of NGQDs. (B) UV–vis and FL spectra of NGQDs. Inset of (B): photograph of NGQDs under visible light and 365 nm UV light, respectively. (C) Dependence of emission of the prepared NGQDs.

(A) The ECL behaviours of GCE in (a) PBS, (b) PBS + NGQDs, (c) PBS + Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and (d) PBS + Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + NGQDs. (B) The stability of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system. (C) UV–vis and (D) FL spectra of (a) Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, (b) NGQDs and (c) Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + NGQDs. (E) FL spectra of (a) PBS + Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, (b) PBS + NGQDs and ECL spectra of (c) their mixture.

(A) DPV curves of GCE (a) PBS, (b) PBS + NGQDs, (c) PBS + Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and (d) PBS + Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + NGQDs. (B) The ECL curves of GCE in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system by SP from 0 V to 1V, 1.1 V, 1.2 V, 1.3 V, 1.4 V and 1.5V. High-resolution XPS of (C) N 1s and (D) C 1s in NGQDs.

(A) Schematic diagram for the construction process of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs based “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor for acetamiprid analysis. (B) EIS and (C) ECL curves of different modified electrodes: GCE, (a) Au/GCE, (b) cDNA/Au/GCE, (c) MCH/cDNA/Au/GCE, (d) Apt/MCH/cDNA/Au/GCE, (e) MB/Apt/MCH/cDNA/Au/GCE and (f) Ace/MB/Apt/MCH/cDNA/Au/GCE.

(A) Regression curve between ΔI and lg cAce. Inset of (A): ECL-Time curves of aptasensors at different concentrations of Ace. (B) Analytical performances of various reported methods and this work for Ace detection. (C) ECL intensity changes of the aptasensors incubated with different interfering pesticides (1 μM Imi, Thi, Chl, Par, and Car respectively; 10 nM Ace; the mixture of 1 μM for each of the five interferents and 10 nM Ace). (D) Repeatability (intra- and inter-assays) of the ECL aptasensor (n = 5), and (E) Stability measurement of the ECL aptasensor for 7 days.
Schematic diagram of the proposed pathway of NGQDs as novel coreactants for enhancing the ECL of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+.
The concentrations of Ace in pakchoi samples measured using the developed ECL aptasensor (n = 3) and the LC-MS method.

head.ce:title: Amplified electrochemiluminescence of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ via coreactant active sites on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Searching for new alternative to tripropylamine (TPrA) with low toxicity and high chemical stability for the tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+) based coreactant electrochemiluminescence (ECL) system is essential for widespread analytical applications. Here, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been discovered to significantly amplify the ECL emission and increase the ECL efficiency of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ for the first time. However, the mechanism by which NGQDs act as coreactants is not well comprehended. Therefore, various optical and electrochemical technologies were employed to investigate the ECL mechanism. It is proposed that the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of NGQDs play crucial roles as the coreactant active sites, catalyzing the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Based on this foundation, an “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor for the quantification of acetamiprid was developed, exhibiting a broad linear range and a detection limit of 0.056 pM. Satisfactory recoveries, ranging from 98.0 % to 101.6 %, were achieved in pakchoi samples. Consequently, NGQDs could serve as coreactants for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, offering new opportunities for constructing a variety of sensors with extensive analytical applications in the ECL field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), an electrochemical redox-induced luminescence process, is widely used owing to its high sensitivity, fast response and low background signal [1–3]. Coreactant ECL is conducive to practical applications, because it can be stimulated by a single directional voltage scan [4]. It is well known that the ECL luminophore employed most frequently is tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) [5,6]. Recently, several studies have focused on the functionalization of Ru(bpy)3 2+ by adding electron-donating or electron-accepting groups at the 4 and 4′ positions of 2,2′-bipyridine (bpy) to alter its physicochemical properties. Specifically, introducing carboxyl groups as σ donors yields tris(4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+), which not only enhances the energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), narrows the HOMO-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap and increases the electron-donating ability of Ru (II), but also facilitates further chemical modification [7–11]. However, as one of the most traditional coreactants in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ ECL system, tripropylamine (TPrA) is toxic, corrosive, poorly soluble in water, and requires high concentrations for good performance [12,13]. Hence, there is an urgent need for an effective alternative to TPrA that has high chemical stability and biocompatibility for a broad range of analytical applications and high ECL efficiency [14,15].

Nanomaterials, including carbon nanomaterials, nitride nanomaterials and II-VI semiconductor, have been adopted as coreactants, which not only enhance ECL performance but also facilitate the development of novel sensing strategies [2,16–18]. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) are a kind of carbon nanomaterial, which have merits of outstanding solubility in water, excellent biocompatibility, stable chemical quality, and excellent photophysical properties [19–21]. Research has proposed that amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on nanomaterials are able to act as coreactant active sites, respectively [14,16,22]. For instance, Carrart et al. successfully proved that amino groups on the surface of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots serve as coreactant active sites, and they can enhance the Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL by a factor of 4 [16]. Recently, Pang's group systematically investigated the effects of carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of carbon nanodots on Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL intensity, revealing that the carboxyl groups on carbon nanodots are the mainly coreactant active sites for Ru(bpy)3 2+ [14]. Consistent with above studies, the abundance of functional groups on the surface of NGQDs may facilitate interesting reactions with Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, potentially significantly influencing ECL emission. Therefore, we hypothesize that suitable NGQDs could be selected as coreactants of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Given consideration of the poor understanding of the ECL enhancement mechanism [14,23], elucidating the correlative mechanisms of NGQDs as coreactants is of significant importance.

In this work, it was discovered that NGQDs synthesized by a simple reflux method under atmospheric pressure not only amplified the ECL emission of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by 11.3-fold with 4-times increased ECL efficiency (
Φ
ECL), but also maintained stable ECL emission under continuous scanning for 50 cycles [19]. Then, to investigate the enhanced ECL mechanism, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy, electrochemistry (EC) and ECL technology were applied. It was demonstrated that NGQDs are efficient coreactants of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+-based ECL by collecting the ECL and FL spectra. Furthermore, the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of NGQDs are proposed to be the active coreactant sites, amplifying the ECL intensity by catalyzing the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. The probable mechanism of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system was discussed. On this basis, we constructed an “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor to detect trace amounts of acetamiprid (Ace), whose excessive residual could potentially harm public health and the environment [24–26]. The constructed aptasensor is capable of sensitively and specifically quantifying Ace in pakchoi.
All materials and apparatus are depicted in Supplementary material.
The fabrication processes are depicted in Supplementary material.

The “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor electrode was immersed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) which contains 0.2 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and 8 mg mL−1 NGQDs. The ECL signal was acquired by performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans at a scanning rate of 0.1 V s−1 from 0 to 1.5 V. A photomultiplier tube high voltage (PMT) of 650 V was applied.

2 g of pakchoi was collected and added into 4 mL of methanol. After oscillating for 10 min and undergoing ultrasonication for 30 min, the sample was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected to obtain the extract solution. Then, the colature was obtained by filtering through filtration membrane. The residual Ace in the extract solution of pakchoi was measured by our sensor and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) method [27,28].

The element composition in NGQDs was explored by XPS. XPS survey spectrum demonstrates that the as-prepared NGQDs are composed of carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s) elements (Fig. 1 A) [19]. The optical properties of NGQDs were explored by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra, which shown in Fig. 1B. The spectrum exhibits a typical absorption peak at 334 nm, indicating that π-π∗ conjugate structure exists in NGQDs [29]. Besides, the FL spectrum exhibits that NGQDs possess robust FL emission at 436 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The inset of Fig. 1B shows that NGQDs appear light yellow under visible light, and emit a bright blue FL under UV light at 365 nm. Different excitation wavelengths were used to further study the optical properties of NGQDs. Fig. 1C illustrates the FL emission peaks of NGQDs are independent of the excitation wavelength, which indicates the surface states are uniform [30]. The above results demonstrate the successful preparation of NGQDs, which are corresponding with the previous report [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL properties of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
ECL technique was used to evaluate the ECL properties of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A, there is no ECL emission when the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is tested in the PBS buffer solution (curve a), which is due to the lack of a luminophore. Similarly, no ECL emission is observed in the PBS buffer solution containing NGQDs (curve b). Subsequently, a weak ECL signal of 600 a.u. is observed upon the introduction of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to the PBS buffer solution (curve c). The addition of NGQDs to the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ solution results in an 11.3-fold enhancement in the ECL signal (curve d), which indicates the ability of NGQDs to amplify the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Simultaneously, the ECL emission barely drops and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 0.8 % under continuous CV scans for 50 cycles, indicating the splendid stability of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, the
Φ ECL
of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system increased by 4-fold compared to that of pure Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, while using Ru(bpy)3 2+/TBABF (
Φ ECL °
= 5 %) as a reference (Table S1) [31]. These results demonstrate that the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ is inefficient, and the introduction of NGQDs can improve the reaction efficiency during the ECL process [32].

To investigate the possible interactions between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NGQDs, optical methods including UV–vis absorbance and FL spectroscopy were employed. As shown in Fig. 2C, Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (curve a) has two obvious characteristic absorption bands at 302 and 470 nm, attributing to intra-ligand π-π∗ transitions and charge transfer between metal center and ligand, respectively [33,34]. NGQDs (curve b) exhibit one characteristic peaks at 334 nm. The absorbance spectrum of their mixture (curve c) is a superposition of absorptions of individual Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NGQDs, indicating that no reaction exists when NGQDs and Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ are at the ground state [35]. However, the FL emission of NGQDs at 436 nm declines significantly upon the addition of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 2D), while the FL emission of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ at 650 nm exhibits a blue shift of 10 nm and increases 5.5-fold compared to that of pure Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, revealing that the excited state of NGQDs may interact with the excited state of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ [33]. Meanwhile, experiments reveal that the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ overlaps well with the FL spectrum of NGQDs (Fig. S1), demonstrating that there may exist resonance energy transfer between the energy donor (NGQDs) and the energy accepter (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+). Furthermore, the ECL spectrum of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs mixture closely resembles the FL spectrum of pure Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 2E) but different from that of NGQDs. Hence, it can be concluded that the luminophore in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system is Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, with NGQDs serving as coreactants according to previous reports [2,17].

For the further investigation of the increasement mechanism that NGQDs as coreactants in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method was applied. The bare GCE exhibits an increasing oxidation current in the PBS buffer solution after 0.9 V (curve a, Fig. 3 A), which is ascribed to the oxygen evolution reaction associated with water oxidation. Similarly, the GCE exhibits no oxidation peak in the PBS buffer solution containing NGQDs (curve b). Subsequently, upon the introduction of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ into the PBS buffer solution, an oxidation peak is observed at 1.14V (curve c). This observation indicates that electrochemical means can inject holes into the HOMO of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to form cation, supporting the prospect of anodic ECL [36]. When NGQDs are introduced into the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ solution, the oxidation peak exhibits a slight negative shift to 1.12 V (curve d), which illustrates that NGQDs can catalyze the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ [35,37]. The electrocatalytic oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs was investigated using a stepping pulse (SP) method. Fig. 3B exhibits the ECL signal with the original stepping potential set to 0 V and the second stepping potential ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 V, respectively. The ECL emission during the second pulse cycles increases from zero to a weak signal (17 a.u.) when the second stepping potential changes from 1.0 to 1.1 V, corresponding to the onset potential (1.02 V) of the oxidation peak of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 3A, curve c). Besides, the ECL signal of second pulse cycles increases quickly in the range from 1.1 V to 1.4 V and stabilizes steady after 1.4 V. The variation trend of the ECL emission is coincident with the oxidation current of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system, which implies that the activation of NGQDs firstly boosts and then stabilizes [37,38].

To further investigate the probable reaction mechanism between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NGQDs, the surface groups and chemical bonding of NGQDs were explored via high-resolution XPS. Fig. 3C reveals XPS spectrum of the N 1s peak of 400.6, 401.2 and 401.9 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic N (CN–C), pyrrolic N (C–N–C) and N–H bond [39]. The peak originating from N–H bond confirms the existence of amino groups in NGQDs. Besides, the five fitted peaks located at 284.5, 285.1, 286.5, 287.5 and 288.6 eV are attributed to the formation of C–C, C–N, C–O, CO and O–CO, indicating the presence of carboxyl groups [19] (Fig. 3D). The above XPS analyses clearly demonstrate that NGQDs contain abundant amino and carboxyl groups, which are proposed to be the coreactant active sites in the ECL emission process of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system, according to previous reports [14,16]. In addition, the influence of solution pH on ECL intensity indicates the deprotonation of NGQDs, as well as the reaction between hydroxide ion and Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ [35] (Fig. S2A). There is almost no difference between the ECL intensity of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system before and after N2 bubbling, indicating that O2 is not participated in the luminescence process [35] (Fig. S2B).

As a result, we propose the mechanism for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+-based ECL with NGQDs as coreactants, which is elaborated by eqs (1)–(8) and described in Scheme 1 . The electrochemical oxidization of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ produced Ru(dcbpy)3 3+(eq (1)). Then, the amino (-NH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups at NGQDs were electrochemical oxidized (eq (2) and eq (5)) and sequentially deprotonated to yield erratic reductive intermediate products: NGQDs-N• (route 1, eq (3)) and NGQDs-COO• (route 2, eq (6)). Next, the reaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ and the erratic reductive intermediate products produced the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+∗(eq (4) and eq (7)). Ru(dcbpy)3 2+∗ returned to the ground state and generated the ECL signal (eq (8)).
(1) Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ − e → Ru(dcbpy)3 3+

Route 1
(2) NGQDs-NH − e → NGQDs-NH+•
(3) NGQDs-NH+• → NGQDs-N• + H+
(4) Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ +NGQDs-N• → Ru(dcbpy)3 2+∗ + Products

Route 2
(5) NGQDs-COOH − e → NGQDs-COOH+•
(6) NGQDs-COOH+• → NGQDs-COO• + H+
(7) Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ +NGQDs-COO• → Ru(dcbpy)3 2+∗ + Products

ECL emission
(8) Ru(dcbpy)3 2+∗ → Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + hν

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characteristic of the “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 A, an “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor was designed base on the outstanding enhancement of NGQDs to the ECL of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. Firstly, Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) were electrodeposited on the surface of GCE in order to immobilize cDNA by Au–S bond. Aptamer (Apt) was paired with cDNA after 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) covering the Au sites, and this state was named as “on” state. Methylene blue (MB) was absorbed into the DNA double strands and utilized as an ECL quencher to reach the “off” state, owing to the ECL-resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) between MB and the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system (Fig. S3) [40]. The presence of Ace can cause the specific combination between Ace and Apt, which result in the release of MB and the realization of the signal “on” state.

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was employed to characterize the stepwise construction of aptasensor (Fig. 4B), with electron transfer resistance (Ret) exhibited by the diameter of the EIS semicircular part. Firstly, the bare GCE shows a very small semicircle diameter (172 Ω), which illustrates the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− diffuse easily. Upon electrodeposition of Au NPs on the electrode surface, Ret decreases to 96 Ω (curve a), demonstrating the good electrical conductivity of Au NPs. Then, there are increases of the Ret after cDNA (curve b, 1044 Ω) and MCH (curve c, 1685 Ω) modifications respectively, which result from the poor electrical conductivity. The addition of Apt reduces Ret to 1084 Ω (curve d). Afterwards, Ret continues decreasing to 350 Ω after MB is adsorbed (curve e). Then Ret increases with the introduction of Ace (curve f, 473 Ω), demonstrating that Ace causes the detachment of Apt and MB. The EIS variation trends are further demonstrated by CV behaviors (Fig. S4). These results confirm that the aptasensor was successfully assembled.

The feasibility of the aptasensor for Ace detection was assessed by analyzing the trend of ECL signals changes (Fig. 4C). The Au/GCE exhibited an ECL signal of 5527 a.u. (curve a). The ECL signal decreased after modifying cDNA (curve b, 5308 a.u.) and MCH (curve c, 3377 a.u.) on the electrode because of their poor electrical conductivity. Upon Apt was attached to the electrode, the ECL signal increased to 4694 a.u. (curve d), which may be due to the double-stranded DNA formed by the aptamer and the cDNA could reduce the barrier to electronic transmission. The ECL signal significantly decreases after absorbing MB (curve e, 323 a.u.). Finally, the addition of Ace triggers specific Ace-Apt binding, and split away from the electrode surface. Meanwhile, part of the combined MB is also released, bringing about the recovery of ECL signal (curve f, 1125 a.u.). Hence, the constructed “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor is feasible for Ace detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the constructed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: For superior analytical performance, experimental parameters such as the incubation time of Apt, MB concentration, binding time between Ace and Apt, and pH of buffer solution were optimized (Fig. S5). Then, the developed aptasensors were analyzed for different concentrations of Ace respectively. The curves in Fig. 5 A implied that ECL emission gradually recovered with Ace concentration increasing. The quantitative study indicated that ΔI (the ECL emission difference of aptasensors before and after identifying Ace) was linearly correlated with the logarithm of the Ace concentration (lg cAce), with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9998 within the range of 1 pM–10 nM (Fig. 5 A). The regression curve equation derived from this was ΔI = 2237 + 177 lg cAce. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.056 pM. Compared with the detection methods which need large-scale instruments and sensors based on other recently developed technology, our sensing platform represented a wider detection range and lower LOD [41–45](Fig. 5B). Compared with previously reported ECL sensors, our sensor exhibited commensurate detection range width and LOD [28,46,47].

To evaluate the detection selectivity, commonly used or structurally similar pesticides imidacloprid (Imi), thiamethoxam (Thi), chlorpyrifos (Chl), parathion-methyl (Par) and carbaryl (Car) were chosen as interferents to conduct the experiment (Fig. 5C). There was almost no ECL intensity change after the addition of Imi, Thi, Chl, Par, and Car respectively. Inversely, whether exposed to Ace or the mixture of the five interferents and Ace, the ECL signal significantly increased. Obviously, the fact that only Ace was able to restore the ECL emission demonstrates the excellent selectivity of the developed aptasensor.

Furthermore, the repeatability of the method was assessed through both intra-assay and inter-assay evaluations. RSD of the ECL intensity of intra-assays is 3.56 % in the same batch, and that of inter-assays is 4.25 % in different batches (Fig. 5D), demonstrating superlative repeatability of the developed aptasensor. When the aptasensors were stored at 4 °C for 7 days, the output signal for the Ace assay showed only a negligible variation, indicating excellent time-stability. (Fig. 5E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of acetamiprid in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor for Ace detection in pakchoi samples was estimated by the standard addition method. As exhibited in Table 1 , the recoveries of the pakchoi are between 98.0 % and 101.6 % with RSD below 5.89 %. The accuracy and reliability of the developed aptasensor were confirmed by the national standard method (LC-MS) for Ace detection. The recoveries obtained by the LC-MS method are between 95.4 % and 107 %. The above results demonstrate the high accuracy and reliability of the aptasensor in effectively quantifying Ace concentrations in real samples.

In summary, NGQDs as coreactants of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ effectively amplify the ECL emission and increase the
Φ
ECL while providing superior stability for this ECL system. Several technologies were utilized to investigate the enhancement mechanism of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system. The coreactant active sites (such as amino and carboxyl groups) of NGQDs could catalyze the oxidation of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, result in significantly influence on the ECL intensity of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system. Eventually, a novel “on-off-on” ECL aptasensor based on the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NGQDs system was designed for Ace quantification. Sensitive analysis of Ace in pakchoi was achieved under the optimum conditions. Besides, the developed aptasensor has good selectivity, excellent repeatability, low detection limit and wide detection range. The amplified ECL of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ based on electrocatalysis from coreactant active sites on NGQDs provides a promising approach for detecting pesticide residue in real samples.

Lingyue Guo: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Libo Li: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Lijun Luo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Tianyan You: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22074055, 22374061, and 62371211), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2023-87), and Jiangsu Province and Education Ministry Co-sponsored Synergistic Innovation Center of Modern Agricultural Equipment (No. XTCX2008).

ce:section.ce:para:
